Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54299.1

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

Zircon inclusions in garnet (ZiG) can be used as an elastic thermobarometer to estimate the pressure-temperature (P-T) conditions of geologic processes. Although this method is being increasingly applied as a thermometer to natural samples, experimental evaluation of ZiG is limited and has not been investigated at geologically relevant pressures. We present the first high-pressure assessment of the reliability of ZiG thermobarometry by crystallizing almandine with zircon inclusions in piston-cylinder experiments between 700−900 °C and 2.0−3.0 GPa. Zircons entrapped in experiments at >2.0 GPa yield residual inclusion pressures within 1σ of predicted values and yield entrapment temperatures within 17−30 °C of experimental T. Zircon inclusions in experiments performed at 2.0 GPa yield average inclusion pressures that are ∼0.09 GPa higher than predicted values and therefore yield entrapment temperatures 65−73 °C higher than the experimental T. Evaluation of inclusion pressure trends shows that ZiG host-inclusion systems did not undergo non-elastic deformation during experimental exhumation, confirming that ZiG elastic thermobarometry can accurately record P-T conditions of garnet crystallization so long as zircon inclusions do not go into tension post-entrapment. Our high-pressure experiments suggest that the ZiG host-inclusion system may be applicable to a variety of geologic settings and processes, such as subduction zone metamorphism, partial melting, and contact metamorphism.

item.title: Structural controls of gold-bearing lodes at the Awanda orogenic gold deposit, Tianshan, NW China
item.dc:creator: Weice Zhao
Xiaobo Zhao
Chunji Xue
Zohreh Rahnama
Xiang Wu
Rongzhen Gao
Ronghao Man
item.description: The Awanda gold deposit (11.6 t Au at average grade of 1.3 g/t) in Xinjiang, NW China, is one of the most important gold discoveries in the Chinese Tianshan over the past 20 years. Gold-bearing lodes of the deposit are confined within the 25-km-long Kalaguole E–W-striking shear zone. Despite its significant control on gold mineralization at Awanda, the structural context and localization of gold-bearing lodes in the shear zone remain poorly understood. In order to elucidate these issues, we herein present systematic structural mapping and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) analysis from the Awanda ore-hosting shear zone. Available data indicate two stages of deformation, including pre-mineral D1-stage north-over-south thrusting kinematics that are characterized by pervasive E–W-striking and N-dipping reverse faults, monoclinal beddings and foliations, and syn-mineral D2-stage locally right-lateral strike-slip deformation that formed the ore-hosting shear zone parallel to the preexisting E–W-striking faults. Deformational mechanism within the shear zone varies from high-temperature (500–650 ℃) grain boundary migration recrystallization in the north to moderate-temperature (400–500 ℃) dislocation creep in the middle, and to low-temperature (300–400 ℃) dislocation glide and climb in the south. The orientation and localization of the Awanda gold-bearing lodes are predominantly subjected to creep dislocation-related moderate-temperature plastic deformation. The right-lateral strike-slip ductile shearing establishes multi-scale pivotal controls on gold mineralization, through extracting ore metals, facilitating fluid flow, and localizing gold-bearing lodes. Published: 2026-03-24T01:40:16+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.571 (consider)

Authors: Weice Zhao; Xiaobo Zhao; Chunji Xue; Zohreh Rahnama; Xiang Wu; Rongzhen Gao; Ronghao Man

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01442-7

Relevance: 0.571 (consider)

The Awanda gold deposit (11.6 t Au at average grade of 1.3 g/t) in Xinjiang, NW China, is one of the most important gold discoveries in the Chinese Tianshan over the past 20 years. Gold-bearing lodes of the deposit are confined within the 25-km-long Kalaguole E–W-striking shear zone. Despite its significant control on gold mineralization at Awanda, the structural context and localization of gold-bearing lodes in the shear zone remain poorly understood. In order to elucidate these issues, we herein present systematic structural mapping and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) analysis from the Awanda ore-hosting shear zone. Available data indicate two stages of deformation, including pre-mineral D1-stage north-over-south thrusting kinematics that are characterized by pervasive E–W-striking and N-dipping reverse faults, monoclinal beddings and foliations, and syn-mineral D2-stage locally right-lateral strike-slip deformation that formed the ore-hosting shear zone parallel to the preexisting E–W-striking faults. Deformational mechanism within the shear zone varies from high-temperature (500–650 ℃) grain boundary migration recrystallization in the north to moderate-temperature (400–500 ℃) dislocation creep in the middle, and to low-temperature (300–400 ℃) dislocation glide and climb in the south. The orientation and localization of the Awanda gold-bearing lodes are predominantly subjected to creep dislocation-related moderate-temperature plastic deformation. The right-lateral strike-slip ductile shearing establishes multi-scale pivotal controls on gold mineralization, through extracting ore metals, facilitating fluid flow, and localizing gold-bearing lodes.

item.title: Copper-hydroxyl interactions drive water-promoted copper surface oxidation and mobility
item.dc:creator: Shuang Li
Zhongliang Cao
Haolong Chang
Wei Zheng
Guoao Luo
Lifeng Zhang
Weiwei Zhang
Langli Luo
Xing Chen
item.description: The reactions of oxygen (O2) and water (H2O) molecules with metal surfaces are critical to heterogeneous catalysis, corrosion, and electrochemical energy conversion. However, disentangling their individual roles remains challenging because both pathways yield the same dissociation product, atomic oxygen (O), and share the hydroxyl (OH) intermediate, thereby obscuring the molecular origin of metal oxidation. In this study, we combine in-situ transmission electron microscopy techniques and ReaxFF reactive force field molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to elucidate the promotional role of H2O in copper (Cu) surface oxidation. Our results reveal that the structurally disordered Cu/CuOx interface preferentially adsorbs OH derived from H2O dissociation. The resulting strong Cu-OH interaction causes dynamic disorder in the topmost Cu layer while enriching electron density in the sublayer. This coupled structural and electronic modulation lowers the resistance for oxygen incorporation, promoting deeper lattice penetration, accelerating oxidation, and enhancing Cu atomic mobility. In contrast, oxidation under pure O2 produces a comparatively ordered interface that suppresses sustained oxygen ingress, rendering further oxidation kinetically less favorable. These findings identify OH-mediated interfacial dynamics as a key driver of water-assisted metal oxidation and provide mechanistic guidance for controlling oxidation processes in catalytic and corrosion-resistant materials. Water and oxygen jointly influence the oxidation of copper, but their respective roles are difficult to disentangle. Here, the authors show that hydroxyl species adsorb at the Cu/CuOx interface, roughen the surface, and enable deeper oxygen penetration and higher Cu mobility. Published: 2026-03-24T02:06:24+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.570 (consider)

The reactions of oxygen (O2) and water (H2O) molecules with metal surfaces are critical to heterogeneous catalysis, corrosion, and electrochemical energy conversion. However, disentangling their individual roles remains challenging because both pathways yield the same dissociation product, atomic oxygen (O), and share the hydroxyl (OH) intermediate, thereby obscuring the molecular origin of metal oxidation. In this study, we combine in-situ transmission electron microscopy techniques and ReaxFF reactive force field molecular dynamics (MD) simulations to elucidate the promotional role of H2O in copper (Cu) surface oxidation. Our results reveal that the structurally disordered Cu/CuOx interface preferentially adsorbs OH derived from H2O dissociation. The resulting strong Cu-OH interaction causes dynamic disorder in the topmost Cu layer while enriching electron density in the sublayer. This coupled structural and electronic modulation lowers the resistance for oxygen incorporation, promoting deeper lattice penetration, accelerating oxidation, and enhancing Cu atomic mobility. In contrast, oxidation under pure O2 produces a comparatively ordered interface that suppresses sustained oxygen ingress, rendering further oxidation kinetically less favorable. These findings identify OH-mediated interfacial dynamics as a key driver of water-assisted metal oxidation and provide mechanistic guidance for controlling oxidation processes in catalytic and corrosion-resistant materials. Water and oxygen jointly influence the oxidation of copper, but their respective roles are difficult to disentangle. Here, the authors show that hydroxyl species adsorb at the Cu/CuOx interface, roughen the surface, and enable deeper oxygen penetration and higher Cu mobility.

item.title: The Variation of the Cenozoic Magmatic Activity Across the Sumatra Region Caused by Wharton Fossil Ridge Subduction
item.dc:creator: Jiawei Li
Qing Liang
Yadan Mao
Chao Chen
Huilin Wang
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Cenozoic igneous rocks south of Sumatra's Toba volcano record distinct magmatic variations across the region. The south underwent prolonged magmatic quiescence, the central region experienced a shorter pause, and the north maintained near continuous activity. Geological and geophysical data link this spatiotemporal variability to changes in slab dip angle, driven by the unevenly buoyant Wharton Ridge. However, the dynamic process of the Wharton Fossil Ridge subduction and the factors that affect it remain unclear. Using “I2VIS” thermomechanical modeling, we investigated how subduction modes depend on ridge cooling age (1–15 Ma) and continental trenchward velocity (1–3.5 cm/yr). Based on the evolution trajectory of slab dip angle ( θ ), we identify three subduction modes: flat‐slab subduction ( θ ≈ 0°), low‐angle decoupled subduction ( θ ≈ 15°), and moderate‐to‐high‐angle subduction ( θ ≥ 30°). Numerical simulations indicate that young oceanic ridges (≤5 Ma) combined with high continental trenchward velocities (≥2 cm/yr) initiate flat‐slab subduction and prolonged magmatic quiescence. In contrast, older ridges (≥7 Ma) and slower trenchward velocities (≤2 cm/yr) promote moderate‐to‐high‐angle subduction and sustained magmatism. Low‐angle decoupled subduction represents an intermediate mode, characterized by shorter periods of quiescence or diminished magmatism. Numerical results align with geological and geophysical observations. Our synthesis indicates that the spatiotemporal variations in Sumatra's magmatism during Cenozoic were jointly influenced by two concurrent factors: the cooling age of the extinct Wharton Ridge as it passed beneath the southern, central, and northern regions, and the clockwise southwestward rotation of the Sumatran Block between 50 and 30 Ma. Published: 2026-03-23T17:25:52+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.570 (consider)

Abstract Cenozoic igneous rocks south of Sumatra's Toba volcano record distinct magmatic variations across the region. The south underwent prolonged magmatic quiescence, the central region experienced a shorter pause, and the north maintained near continuous activity. However, the dynamic process of the Wharton Fossil Ridge subduction and the factors that affect it remain unclear. Using “I2VIS” thermomechanical modeling, we investigated how subduction modes depend on ridge cooling age (1–15 Ma) and continental trenchward velocity (1–3.5 cm/yr). Based on the evolution trajectory of slab dip angle ( θ ), we identify three subduction modes: flat‐slab subduction ( θ ≈ 0°), low‐angle decoupled subduction ( θ ≈ 15°), and moderate‐to‐high‐angle subduction ( θ ≥ 30°). Numerical simulations indicate that young oceanic ridges (≤5 Ma) combined with high continental trenchward velocities (≥2 cm/yr) initiate flat‐slab subduction and prolonged magmatic quiescence. In contrast, older ridges (≥7 Ma) and slower trenchward velocities (≤2 cm/yr) promote moderate‐to‐high‐angle subduction and sustained magmatism. Low‐angle decoupled subduction represents an intermediate mode, characterized by shorter periods of quiescence or diminished magmatism. Numerical results align with geological and geophysical observations. Our synthesis indicates that the spatiotemporal variations in Sumatra's magmatism during Cenozoic were jointly influenced by two concurrent factors: the cooling age of the extinct Wharton Ridge as it passed beneath the southern, central, and northern regions, and the clockwise southwestward rotation of the Sumatran Block between 50 and 30 Ma.

item.title: Extensive fractional crystallization in strongly peraluminous granitoids recorded by zircon Zr isotopes
item.dc:creator: Ze Liu
Nicole Xike Nie
Oliver Jagoutz
Santiago Tassara
Jay J. Ague
Jin-Cheng Xie
Liang-Liang Zhang
Qing Wang
Di-Cheng Zhu
item.description: Strongly peraluminous granitoids, classically considered “S-type” melts derived from metasedimentary sources, often exhibit large compositional variations, particularly in their alumina saturation and maficity. Such variations are generally attributed to (1) selective entrainment and/or unmixing of restitic and/or peritectic minerals in anatectic melts or (2) fractional crystallization (FC) of a parental magma. Distinguishing between these two models solely based on chemical compositions is challenging. Here, we use zircon Zr stable isotopes ( δ 94/90 Zr relative to NIST RM 8299) to investigate the petrogenesis of the archetypal Kinsman granitoids and associated ultramafic–mafic cumulates from the New England Appalachians, USA. Autocrystic zircon from the Kinsman granitoids records a ∼ 0.3‰ increase in δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (from –0.23 to +0.12‰) that correlates with increasing whole-rock SiO 2 (58–76 wt%). This systematic isotopic evolution, which is decoupled from zircon ε Hf (t), negatively correlates with magma temperature (688–812°C). These characteristics argue against open-system processes such as restite entrainment or continuous crustal assimilation during magma ascent in the crust. Instead, they suggest a nearly closed-system evolution dominated by FC. The lower δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (–0.45 to –0.04‰) of zircon from associated olivine-pyroxene hornblendites and orthopyroxenites are consistent with their origin as earlier cumulates genetically linked to the Kinsman granitoids. Our results demonstrate that while sediment assimilation in the lower crust could be responsible for the peraluminous signature, FC is the dominant driver of compositional diversity in these systems. Published: 2026-03-21T23:29:37+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.567 (consider)

Authors: Ze Liu; Nicole Xike Nie; Oliver Jagoutz; Santiago Tassara; Jay J. Ague; Jin-Cheng Xie; Liang-Liang Zhang; Qing Wang; Di-Cheng Zhu

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.034

Relevance: 0.567 (consider)

Strongly peraluminous granitoids, classically considered “S-type” melts derived from metasedimentary sources, often exhibit large compositional variations, particularly in their alumina saturation and maficity. Such variations are generally attributed to (1) selective entrainment and/or unmixing of restitic and/or peritectic minerals in anatectic melts or (2) fractional crystallization (FC) of a parental magma. Distinguishing between these two models solely based on chemical compositions is challenging. Here, we use zircon Zr stable isotopes ( δ 94/90 Zr relative to NIST RM 8299) to investigate the petrogenesis of the archetypal Kinsman granitoids and associated ultramafic–mafic cumulates from the New England Appalachians, USA. Autocrystic zircon from the Kinsman granitoids records a ∼ 0.3‰ increase in δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (from –0.23 to +0.12‰) that correlates with increasing whole-rock SiO 2 (58–76 wt%). This systematic isotopic evolution, which is decoupled from zircon ε Hf (t), negatively correlates with magma temperature (688–812°C). These characteristics argue against open-system processes such as restite entrainment or continuous crustal assimilation during magma ascent in the crust. Instead, they suggest a nearly closed-system evolution dominated by FC. The lower δ 94/90 Zr NIST values (–0.45 to –0.04‰) of zircon from associated olivine-pyroxene hornblendites and orthopyroxenites are consistent with their origin as earlier cumulates genetically linked to the Kinsman granitoids. Our results demonstrate that while sediment assimilation in the lower crust could be responsible for the peraluminous signature, FC is the dominant driver of compositional diversity in these systems.

item.title: Ophiolites in Pakistan: Key evidence for the geodynamic evolution of the Neo-Tethys Ocean
item.dc:creator: Inayat Ullah
Peng-Fei Zhang
Wen-Jun Hu
Mei-Fu Zhou
item.description: In Pakistan, two major Late Mesozoic Neo-Tethyan suture zones, the Western Suture Zone (WSZ) and Indus Suture Zone (ISZ), delineate the remnants of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean. These suture zones and associated ophiolites document the rift-drift history, northward migration of the Indian Plate, and geodynamic evolution of the India-Eurasia collision. This review aims to refine the geodynamic model of Pakistani ophiolites and contribute to the reconstruction of Neo-Tethyan tectonic evolution and the Alpine-Himalayan orogenic system. The WSZ ophiolites, located along the west-northwest boundary of the Indian Plate, contains well-preserved Penrose-style sequences, such as those near Bela, Muslim Bagh, and Waziristan. These ophiolites are interpreted to be formed first at ridge-like spreading centers and later in supra-subduction zones (SSZ) during the northwestward subduction of the Neo-Tethys oceanic slab beneath the southern margin of the Eurasian Plate during subduction initiation Cretaceous. The ISZ comprises highly fragmented ophiolitic mélanges, including the Dargai and Shangla-Mingora complexes, which reflect complex intra-oceanic subduction, accretion, and collision processes along the Kohistan Island Arc. These sutures record the tectonic evolution of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean, with the complied petrological, geochemical, and geochronological data indicating a transition initially from mid-ocean ridge basalt (MORB) to island-arc tholeiite (IAT). The geochemical diversity in their mantle sections, ranging from MORB to IAT and boninitic signatures, reflects varied magmatic processes during subduction initiation. The emplacement of these ophiolites, influenced by oblique convergence and transpression in the WSZ and by collision-driven accretion in the ISZ, illustrates the complex tectonics associated with closure of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean. Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

In Pakistan, two major Late Mesozoic Neo-Tethyan suture zones, the Western Suture Zone (WSZ) and Indus Suture Zone (ISZ), delineate the remnants of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean. These suture zones and associated ophiolites document the rift-drift history, northward migration of the Indian Plate, and geodynamic evolution of the India-Eurasia collision. This review aims to refine the geodynamic model of Pakistani ophiolites and contribute to the reconstruction of Neo-Tethyan tectonic evolution and the Alpine-Himalayan orogenic system. The WSZ ophiolites, located along the west-northwest boundary of the Indian Plate, contains well-preserved Penrose-style sequences, such as those near Bela, Muslim Bagh, and Waziristan. These ophiolites are interpreted to be formed first at ridge-like spreading centers and later in supra-subduction zones (SSZ) during the northwestward subduction of the Neo-Tethys oceanic slab beneath the southern margin of the Eurasian Plate during subduction initiation Cretaceous. The ISZ comprises highly fragmented ophiolitic mélanges, including the Dargai and Shangla-Mingora complexes, which reflect complex intra-oceanic subduction, accretion, and collision processes along the Kohistan Island Arc. These sutures record the tectonic evolution of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean, with the complied petrological, geochemical, and geochronological data indicating a transition initially from mid-ocean ridge basalt (MORB) to island-arc tholeiite (IAT). The geochemical diversity in their mantle sections, ranging from MORB to IAT and boninitic signatures, reflects varied magmatic processes during subduction initiation. The emplacement of these ophiolites, influenced by oblique convergence and transpression in the WSZ and by collision-driven accretion in the ISZ, illustrates the complex tectonics associated with closure of the Neo-Tethyan Ocean.

item.title: In-situ mineral chemistry, sulfur and lead isotopes, and paragenetic analysis to unravel the ore-forming processes in Qibaoshan Co-Ni-Cu-Pb-Zn deposit (Qin-Hang metallogenic belt, South China)
item.dc:creator: Yao-Ming Xu
Shao-Yong Jiang
Zhen Yin
Wei-Xin Zhang
Hong-Li Yi
item.description: Abstract Cobalt and nickel are critical metals in high-demand for clean energy technologies, its occurrence in sediment-hosted deposits is mostly in association with Cu, but rare in association with Pb-Zn mineralization. The Qibaoshan deposit, located in the Qin-Hang metallogenic belt of western Jiangxi, South China, presents a unique case of stratiform Zn-Pb-Co-Ni-Cu mineralization hosted in Devonian-Carboniferous clastic-carbonate strata, with no exposed magmatic rocks. This study integrates in-situ mineral chemistry, sulfur and lead isotopes, and paragenetic analysis to unravel the ore-forming processes of this unusual polymetallic system. Results reveal two distinct mineralization events: (1) an earlier Pb-Zn stage driven by basin fluids heated by concealed magmatism, with metals and sulfur sourced from the metamorphic basement; and (2) a later Co-Cu-Ni stage linked to intermediate-mafic magmatic-hydrothermal fluids, introducing magma-derived sulfur and metals. Cobalt and nickel occur mainly as independent minerals, including sulfarsenide cobaltite, gersdorffite, diarsenide safflorite, rammelsbergite, loellingite, and triarsenide skutterudite, with minor substitution in pyrite and arsenopyrite. This study highlights a rare example of superimposed sediment-hosted base metal and critical metal mineralization, driven by dual fluid systems with contrasting sources. The findings challenge traditional models of sediment-hosted deposits and offer new insights into the diversity of cobalt enrichment mechanisms. The Qibaoshan deposit serves as a key natural laboratory for understanding metal coexistence, fluid evolution, and exploration targeting in complex polymetallic systems. Published: 2026-03-25T20:00:51+00:00 Venue: American Mineralogist Score: 0.562 (consider)

Abstract Cobalt and nickel are critical metals in high-demand for clean energy technologies, its occurrence in sediment-hosted deposits is mostly in association with Cu, but rare in association with Pb-Zn mineralization. The Qibaoshan deposit, located in the Qin-Hang metallogenic belt of western Jiangxi, South China, presents a unique case of stratiform Zn-Pb-Co-Ni-Cu mineralization hosted in Devonian-Carboniferous clastic-carbonate strata, with no exposed magmatic rocks. This study integrates in-situ mineral chemistry, sulfur and lead isotopes, and paragenetic analysis to unravel the ore-forming processes of this unusual polymetallic system. Results reveal two distinct mineralization events: (1) an earlier Pb-Zn stage driven by basin fluids heated by concealed magmatism, with metals and sulfur sourced from the metamorphic basement; and (2) a later Co-Cu-Ni stage linked to intermediate-mafic magmatic-hydrothermal fluids, introducing magma-derived sulfur and metals. Cobalt and nickel occur mainly as independent minerals, including sulfarsenide cobaltite, gersdorffite, diarsenide safflorite, rammelsbergite, loellingite, and triarsenide skutterudite, with minor substitution in pyrite and arsenopyrite. This study highlights a rare example of superimposed sediment-hosted base metal and critical metal mineralization, driven by dual fluid systems with contrasting sources. The findings challenge traditional models of sediment-hosted deposits and offer new insights into the diversity of cobalt enrichment mechanisms. The Qibaoshan deposit serves as a key natural laboratory for understanding metal coexistence, fluid evolution, and exploration targeting in complex polymetallic systems.

item.title: Tropical mafic rock weathering, riverine lithium isotopes, and the evolution of Cenozoic seawater chemistry
item.dc:creator: Chris T.L. Cheung
Kohen W. Bauer
Brian Beaty
Juan Miguel Guotana
Decibel V. Faustino-Eslava
Boriana Kalderon-Asael
Dan Asael
Karen Luk
Giancarlo DeFrancesco
Sean M. Newby
Yonghui Qin
Noah J. Planavsky
N. Ryan McKenzie
item.description: The terrestrial dissolved riverine lithium isotope (δ 7 Li diss ) flux can provide valuable information on silicate weathering processes, which are directly tied to the long-term carbon cycle, at various spatiotemporal scales. However, a limited understanding of how bedrock lithological composition impacts δ 7 Li diss values hinders the utility of the Li proxy for deep-time weathering reconstructions. Accordingly, we investigated tropical rivers in the Philippines and Mariana Islands that drain either distinctly ultramafic-to-mafic or silicic bedrock. We find that mafic lithologies produce markedly heavier δ 7 Li diss than those draining silicic lithologies, potentially due to preferential formation of Mg-rich clays during incongruent weathering. Furthermore, we observe that known tectonically driven changes in Earth’s global ophiolitic-collisional suture length correlate with the marine δ 7 Li record over the past ∼60 million years. This observation, supported by mass-balance modelling, indicates that variations in mafic bedrock weathering could have been an important influence on Cenozoic seawater chemistry. Published: 2026-03-19T14:12:32+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.559 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chris T.L. Cheung; Kohen W. Bauer; Brian Beaty; Juan Miguel Guotana; Decibel V. Faustino-Eslava; Boriana Kalderon-Asael; Dan Asael; Karen Luk; Giancarlo DeFrancesco; Sean M. Newby; Yonghui Qin; Noah J. Planavsky; N. Ryan McKenzie

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.119917

Relevance: 0.559 (consider)

The terrestrial dissolved riverine lithium isotope (δ 7 Li diss ) flux can provide valuable information on silicate weathering processes, which are directly tied to the long-term carbon cycle, at various spatiotemporal scales. However, a limited understanding of how bedrock lithological composition impacts δ 7 Li diss values hinders the utility of the Li proxy for deep-time weathering reconstructions. Accordingly, we investigated tropical rivers in the Philippines and Mariana Islands that drain either distinctly ultramafic-to-mafic or silicic bedrock. We find that mafic lithologies produce markedly heavier δ 7 Li diss than those draining silicic lithologies, potentially due to preferential formation of Mg-rich clays during incongruent weathering. Furthermore, we observe that known tectonically driven changes in Earth’s global ophiolitic-collisional suture length correlate with the marine δ 7 Li record over the past ∼60 million years. This observation, supported by mass-balance modelling, indicates that variations in mafic bedrock weathering could have been an important influence on Cenozoic seawater chemistry.

item.title: Identifying ca. 190 Ma Nb-Ta-Sn metasomatic alteration in the world-class Bailongshan pegmatite Li deposit, Northwest China
item.dc:creator: Tao Hong
Zhang Zhang
Ming-Guo Zhai
Hai-Yang Xian
Yuan Xue
He Huang
Xing-Wang Xu
item.description: Pegmatite-aplite systems are critical hosts for Li, Nb, and Ta mineralization, yet the role of their spatial distribution and overprinting enrichment processes remains poorly constrained. The world-class Bailongshan Li pegmatite deposit (Li2O resource >5 Mt), situated in the Western Kunlun Orogenic Belt of Northwest China, hosts Li-Nb-Ta mineralization primarily in coarse-grained spodumene pegmatites (Spd-1; >3 cm wide; pegmatite layer). These Spd-1 pegmatites are crosscut by fine-grained, spodumene-bearing pegmatites (Spd-2; <3 cm wide; aplite layer) that also exhibit distinct Nb-Ta-Sn enrichment. Two types of aplite are distinguished in the Bailongshan deposit: (1) aplite associated with and parallel to pegmatite zones, which is mostly spodumene-free, and (2) aplite layers that crosscut pegmatite zones at a small angle, exhibit a fine-grained texture, host fine-grained spodumene, and are characterized by typical hydrothermal alteration. Geochronology of columbite-group minerals (CGMs) in the coarse-grained pegmatite and fine-grained aplite layers reveals primary magmatic crystallization at ca. 210 Ma and a later metasomatic alteration at ca. 190 Ma (191.8 ± 1.5 Ma to ca. 191.0 ± 1.4 Ma), recorded by CGMs with higher Sn, Hf, Zr, and total rare earth elements (ΣREEs) with dissolution-precipitation textures. Spd-2 crystals in the fine-grained spodumene pegmatite are primarily xenocrystic, as the structural data obtained from single-crystal X-ray diffraction of both coarse-grained and fine-grained spodumene exhibits comparable structural characteristics. The electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) data indicated a slight orientation difference (0° to 2°) between the Spd-2 grains and the larger Spd-1 crystals. Coarse-grained Spd-1 was incorporated into the fine-grained aplite layer to form the xenocrystic Spd-2; this is evidenced by their comparable crystallographic structures despite the elevated Na, Mn, Fe, Ga, and Sn concentrations in Spd-2. The “bookshelf” structures in the aplite layer further suggest that ductile deformation facilitated the mechanical disaggregation of spodumene; this, combined with metasomatic alteration, contributed to localized Nb-Ta-Sn enrichment. Published: 2026-03-25T20:46:23+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.558 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tao Hong; Zhang Zhang; Ming-Guo Zhai; Hai-Yang Xian; Yuan Xue; He Huang; Xing-Wang Xu

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38620.1

Relevance: 0.558 (consider)

Pegmatite-aplite systems are critical hosts for Li, Nb, and Ta mineralization, yet the role of their spatial distribution and overprinting enrichment processes remains poorly constrained. The world-class Bailongshan Li pegmatite deposit (Li2O resource >5 Mt), situated in the Western Kunlun Orogenic Belt of Northwest China, hosts Li-Nb-Ta mineralization primarily in coarse-grained spodumene pegmatites (Spd-1; >3 cm wide; pegmatite layer). These Spd-1 pegmatites are crosscut by fine-grained, spodumene-bearing pegmatites (Spd-2; <3 cm wide; aplite layer) that also exhibit distinct Nb-Ta-Sn enrichment. Two types of aplite are distinguished in the Bailongshan deposit: (1) aplite associated with and parallel to pegmatite zones, which is mostly spodumene-free, and (2) aplite layers that crosscut pegmatite zones at a small angle, exhibit a fine-grained texture, host fine-grained spodumene, and are characterized by typical hydrothermal alteration. Geochronology of columbite-group minerals (CGMs) in the coarse-grained pegmatite and fine-grained aplite layers reveals primary magmatic crystallization at ca. 210 Ma and a later metasomatic alteration at ca. 190 Ma (191.8 ± 1.5 Ma to ca. 191.0 ± 1.4 Ma), recorded by CGMs with higher Sn, Hf, Zr, and total rare earth elements (ΣREEs) with dissolution-precipitation textures. Spd-2 crystals in the fine-grained spodumene pegmatite are primarily xenocrystic, as the structural data obtained from single-crystal X-ray diffraction of both coarse-grained and fine-grained spodumene exhibits comparable structural characteristics. The electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) data indicated a slight orientation difference (0° to 2°) between the Spd-2 grains and the larger Spd-1 crystals. Coarse-grained Spd-1 was incorporated into the fine-grained aplite layer to form the xenocrystic Spd-2; this is evidenced by their comparable crystallographic structures despite the elevated Na, Mn, Fe, Ga, and Sn concentrations in Spd-2. The “bookshelf” structures in the aplite layer further suggest that ductile deformation facilitated the mechanical disaggregation of spodumene; this, combined with metasomatic alteration, contributed to localized Nb-Ta-Sn enrichment.
